cases.name: North Carolina v. Butler
cases.holding: An express written or oral waiver is not required; a valid Miranda waiver may be inferred from the suspect's words and conduct — but silence alone is never enough, and the burden stays on the government.

cases.name: Berghuis v. Thompkins
cases.holding: The right to remain silent must be invoked UNAMBIGUOUSLY; merely staying silent does not invoke it, and a suspect who answers questions after a long silence has impliedly waived the right.
cases.treatment_note: Current; invocation of the right to silence must be unambiguous and waiver may be implied. No negative treatment.

cases.name: Edwards v. Arizona
cases.holding: Once an accused invokes his Fifth Amendment right to counsel, police may not reinitiate interrogation until counsel has been made available, unless the accused himself initiates further communication.

quotes.text: We further hold that an accused, such as Edwards, having expressed his desire to deal with the police only through counsel, is not subject to further interrogation by the authorities until counsel has been made available to him, unless the accused himself initiates further communication, exchanges, or conversations with the police.

cases.name: Michigan v. Mosley
cases.holding: After a suspect invokes the right to SILENCE, later statements are admissible if police "scrupulously honored" the invocation — here, questioning ceased, time passed, fresh warnings were given, and the second interrogation concerned a different crime.

cases.name: Davis v. United States
cases.holding: A suspect must invoke the right to counsel UNAMBIGUOUSLY; an equivocal or ambiguous reference ("maybe I should talk to a lawyer") does not require police to stop questioning, and they need not even ask clarifying questions.

quotes.text: But if a suspect makes a reference to an attorney that is ambiguous or equivocal in that a reasonable officer in light of the circumstances would have understood only that the suspect might be invoking the right to counsel, our precedents do not require the cessation of questioning.

cases.name: Maryland v. Shatzer
cases.holding: Edwards protection ends after a 14-day break in Miranda custody; once 14 days pass, police may re-approach and seek a fresh waiver. Release back into the general prison population counts as a break in custody.

cases.name: Oregon v. Elstad
cases.holding: An initial, un-warned but voluntary statement does not automatically taint a later confession; if the suspect is then properly Mirandized and waives his rights, the second, warned statement is admissible.

cases.name: Arizona v. Roberson
cases.holding: The Edwards bar is not offense-specific — once a suspect invokes counsel, police may not interrogate him about ANY offense, including an unrelated one, without counsel present (and a second officer's ignorance of the invocation is no excuse).

cases.name: Minnick v. Mississippi
cases.holding: Once counsel is invoked, Edwards bars police-initiated re-interrogation without counsel PRESENT — and that protection is not satisfied merely because the suspect has already consulted with an attorney.

cases.name: Moran v. Burbine
cases.holding: A Miranda waiver is valid even though police failed to tell the suspect that an attorney was trying to reach him; events outside the suspect's knowledge don't bear on whether HE knowingly and voluntarily waived his rights.

note: Phase-3 FINAL authoring manifest: packet(manifest+deck) + P3 gap verifications + marquee quotes + good-law.
